fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.

A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
A type A is called a set when for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is a proposition.
The notions of contractible and propositional types generate an infinite hierarchy of truncation levels expressing the point after which higher identifications become trivial.
A truncation level is an integer n \geq -2.
Given a truncation level n, we define what it means for a type to be n-truncated by induction:

fr:ul.fr:li: a type is -2-truncated when it is contractible;
a type is -1-truncated when it is a proposition;
for n \geq 0, a type A is n-truncated when for every x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-1-truncated.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: So, in particular, a 0-truncated type is precisely a set. We shall often refer to n-truncated types as n-types.

A function f\colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as propositional when their fibers are propositions.

fr:title.text: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition ›

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition ›

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: By Lemma , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in Example :
It is not difficult to show using Definition that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.
If A is a proposition, then for all x,y:A the identification type x=y is contractible.
The centre of contraction of x=y is given by our assumption that A is a proposition; the contracting identifications are obtained from the same assumption via Lemma .
We must show that each fiber of f is a proposition; in fact, each fiber is contractible as biinvertible maps are contractible, and contractible types are propositions.

Fixing b:B, we must check that the fiber of f over b is contractible. For the centre of contraction, we must construct an element of this fiber; because f is propositional by Lemma , the fiber is a proposition, so it suffices by the induction principle for propositional truncation to observe that the fiber is inhabited because f is surjective.

fr:title.text: Contractible types are closed under retracts

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contractible types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If B is a retract of A and A is contractible, then B is a contractible.
Let s\colon B \to A be a section of r\colon A\to B; if a_0 is the centre of contraction of A, then the centre of contraction of B is given by ra_0. For the rest, we use Lemma and Lemma .
When we have an element of \mathsf {toProp}\,A, we will say that A is inhabited.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products (Lemma ) via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .
As A is contractible with some centre a:A, we have for any x,y:A identifications \chi _x \colon a = x and \chi _y\colon a = y. Therefore, we identify x with y using \chi _x^{-1} \cdot \chi _y.
We proceed by induction on the truncation level n.

fr:ol.fr:li: When n=-2, we recall from Lemma that being contractible is a proposition.
When n=-1, we recall from Lemma that being a proposition is a proposition.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
A type A is called a set when for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is a proposition.
The notions of contractible and propositional types generate an infinite hierarchy of truncation levels expressing the point after which higher identifications become trivial.
A truncation level is an integer n \geq -2.
Given a truncation level n, we define what it means for a type to be n-truncated by induction:

fr:ul.fr:li: a type is -2-truncated when it is contractible;
a type is -1-truncated when it is a proposition;
for n \geq 0, a type A is n-truncated when for every x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-1-truncated.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: So, in particular, a 0-truncated type is precisely a set. We shall often refer to n-truncated types as n-types.

A function f\colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as propositional when their fibers are propositions.
It is a proposition that a given type A is contractible.
The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce from Lemma and Lemma .
If a type A is contractible, then it is a proposition.
As A is contractible with some centre a:A, we have for any x,y:A identifications \chi _x \colon a = x and \chi _y\colon a = y. Therefore, we identify x with y using \chi _x^{-1} \cdot \chi _y.
This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
Given a truncation level n, we define what it means for a type to be n-truncated by induction:

fr:ul.fr:li: a type is -2-truncated when it is contractible;
a type is -1-truncated when it is a proposition;
for n \geq 0, a type A is n-truncated when for every x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-1-truncated.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: So, in particular, a 0-truncated type is precisely a set. We shall often refer to n-truncated types as n-types.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.

Let \chi _x \colon a = x be the witness of contraction for any x:A. Given b : Ba and x:A, we define \mathsf {ind}_{A,a}\,b\,x : Bx by transporting b along the identification \chi _a^{-1} \cdot \chi _x \colon a = x:

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{C}\colon C\cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by Corollary . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (Axiom , Axiom ):
By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{C}\colon C\cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by Corollary . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (Axiom , Axiom ):
By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.
